text: This video demonstrates single sitting robotic retroperitoneal pelvic and inguinal lymphadenectomy.
A fifty seven year old gentleman presented with enlarged hard and fixed right inguinal lymph nodes. The true cut biopsy revealed poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma.
He had a prior history of excision of eccrine carcinoma of the right flank in two thousand nine. He was disease free on yearly follow ups.

A PET-CT scan was done, which revealed metastasis in parietic atriocaval, retrocaval, paracavel, right common iliac, right inguinal, and left cervical lymph nodes. The cytology from left cervical node was suggestive of squamous carcinomatous deposits.

In view of the good performance status of the patient and positive treatment guidelines for this extremely rare neoplasm, it was decided to proceed with simultaneous robotic retroperitoneal right pelvic and right inguinal lymphadenectomy. An informed consent was obtained from the patient regarding the non-curative nature of the surgery.

The da Vinci X-ray system is utilized for the procedure, with the patient positioned in the Trendelenburg position for the retroperitoneal and pelvic surgery, and a split leg position for the inguinal lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon places ports in a horizontal straight line for the bilateral retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND), with a 12mm assistant port on the right side and another 12mm port in the suprapubic area over the midline to assist in the left RPLND and manage potential vascular injuries.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum medial to the cecum and lifts the peritoneal folds using external nylon sutures loaded on straight needles. The dissection progresses to the right common iliac lymph nodes, extending up to the vena cava.
A rent on the anterior aspect of the vena cava is visible, which is being repaired using 3-0 Prolene sutures. Following the repair, the dissection continues cranially along the vena cava.
The surgeon uses the split and roll method to separate the lymph nodes from the major vessels, while the lumbar vessels are divided between hemlock clips.

The surgeon performs en bloc dissection and removal of the paracaval lymph nodes. Subsequently, the intra-aortocaval and pre-aortic lymph nodes are dissected. During this process, an injury occurs to the left renal artery at the ostium level.

The surgeon uses the left robotic instrument to apply compression on the left renal artery while repairing the rent with a rescue stitch using a 3-0 Prolene suture. An intraoperative Doppler confirms pulsatile flow in the left renal artery following the repair.
The surgeon divides small vessels and lymphatic channels between clips, while further dissecting the para-aortic lymph nodes using robotic instruments.
The surgeon continues dissection using robotic instruments, inadvertently injuring the inferior mesenteric artery. The artery is clipped, and its ostium is repaired with a 3-0 proline rescue stitch.

The surgeon loops the aorta and dissects the inter-aortocaval lymph nodes, proceeding cranially to identify the right renal artery. The inter-aortocaval lymph nodes are removed, followed by deeper dissection to excise the retrocaval and paracaval lymph nodes. The final view reveals the great vessels after the completed retroperitoneal dissection.

The surgical boom is rotated towards the right pelvis using a 30-degree up camera. An extended right pelvic lymph node dissection is performed, with grossly enlarged lymph nodes around the iliac artery and vein being dissected and removed. The dissection continues distally over the iliac vessels, culminating in a final view of the right pelvis after the completed lymphadenectomy.

The robotic system is repositioned to subcutaneous ports for the right inguinal lymphadenectomy. The dissection begins superficially to the fascia lata up to the inguinal ligament. The fascia lata is opened to access the femoral vessels, where the tributaries and main trunk of the great saphenous vein are divided. An accidental tear in the right femoral artery is repaired using a three-oh proline rescue stitch.

The surgeon performs cranial dissection while carefully avoiding thinning and button-holing of the skin. The lymph node resembling a clock weight is removed and placed into a specimen bag. The femoral ring is then closed.

The total consult time was four sixty-five minutes and the estimated blood loss was three hundred milliliters. The patient did not require any intraoperative or postoperative blood transfusion. He was discharged on the third postoperative day with a groin drain which was removed after twelve days. Seventy-eight out of the seventy-nine lymph nodes removed were positive for poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma.
The patient was started on cycloposphamide, adriamycin, cisplatin chemotherapy and is doing well at two months of follow-up.

This video is presented to emphasize the minimally invasive management of vascular complications of retroperitoneal lymphadenectomy and inguinal lymphadenectomy. These complications can be very well managed using the robot without compromising the oncological outcomes. The importance of a solid and skilled robotic team cannot be underestimated.
